title: Iodine on Cotton — Sodium Thiosulfate Reduction
stainChemistry: Iodine forms charge-transfer complexes with cellulose fibers, creating a deep blue-black stain especially in the presence of starch. The iodine (I₂) physically absorbs into the cotton's cellulose matrix and forms van der Waals bonds with hydroxyl groups on the fiber chains. This is not a true dye bond but a molecular complex that penetrates the fiber.
whyThisWorks: Sodium thiosulfate (hypo) reduces molecular iodine (I₂) to colorless iodide ion (I⁻) through a redox reaction: I₂ + 2S₂O₃²⁻ → 2I⁻ + S₄O₆²⁻. The iodide is highly soluble and washes away. Any residual brown staining from oxidized iodine compounds is then removed with hydrogen peroxide 3%, which oxidizes iodide back to soluble iodine while bleaching any remaining discoloration.

spottingProtocol.agent: sodium thiosulfate solution (1 oz hypo per 16 oz water, tepid)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, gentle pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge the thiosulfate solution into the stain from the back side. Work from outside the stain inward to prevent spreading. Keep the area wet and repeatedly sponge until the blue-black color begins to fade. Replace the under-pad as it absorbs the stain.

After thiosulfate treatment, sponge with hydrogen peroxide 3% to oxidize any remaining brown iodine residue and complete the bleaching action. This step turns any remaining stain colorless. Work gently to avoid over-bleaching adjacent fibers.

spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with distilled water to remove all chemical residue. Sponge repeatedly with fresh water and clean pad until no chemical smell remains. Blot excess water.

spottingProtocol.agent: 5% acetic acid solution (white vinegar diluted 1:3 with water)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final sponge with diluted acetic acid to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and restore fiber pH. This prevents yellowing.

escalation: If stain persists after thiosulfate and peroxide treatment, do not repeat. Iodine may have formed insoluble compounds deep in the fiber. Professional dry-clean solvent bath with detergent additive may help. If the stain is set and brown, it may be permanent.
commonMistakes: Using chlorine bleach instead of sodium thiosulfate — chlorine oxidizes iodine and makes stain darker.
Applying thiosulfate to wet fabric — the solution dilutes and loses potency.
Assuming all brown residue is iodine — some may be fiber damage that cannot be removed.
scienceNote: The charge-transfer complex between iodine and cellulose explains why iodine stains are so color-stable. The reduction mechanism (thiosulfate) is the key — it's the same chemistry used in photography to fix film (removing unreacted silver halide). This is why the Eisen method prioritizes understanding oxidation-reduction reactions in spotting.
safety: Sodium thiosulfate is non-toxic and mild. Hydrogen peroxide 3% is safe on cotton. No PPE required beyond standard spotting gloves. Test hydrogen peroxide on an inconspicuous area first to check for color fastness — some dyes may bleach.
materialWarnings: Cotton is stable with all reagents used.
Test on seams or interior hems if garment has been previously dyed, as hydrogen peroxide can bleach some dyes.
callPro: If the stain is brown, older than 48 hours, or if the fabric is a delicate weave, professional treatment is safer. Repeated amateur spotting can weaken cotton fibers.
customerExplanation: Iodine stains create a chemical complex with cotton fibers. We used sodium thiosulfate, a reducing agent, to convert the iodine into a soluble form that washes away. A final hydrogen peroxide treatment removed any residual brown discoloration. The garment is now restored.

homeSolutions.agent: Iodine is a complex — sodium thiosulfate dissolves it
homeSolutions.instruction: Iodine (Betadine, tincture of iodine, povidone-iodine) stains cotton through a chemical complex, not a dye bond. The specific tool that breaks that complex is sodium thiosulfate — sold as "hypo" at photography stores or as chlorine neutralizer at pool-supply stores (under $10). A small bottle solves this stain. Without it: vinegar, milk, and baking soda will all give minor improvements but nothing will fully lift the orange/brown. Potato starch pulled from fresh spills only. If you have thiosulfate, you're in good shape.

Dab a drop of cold water on an inside seam, wait 60 seconds, blot. If the fabric holds its color, proceed. Turn the garment inside out and flush cold water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Iodine is water-soluble while fresh — this flush alone often removes 50% of the pigment on fresh stains.

homeSolutions.agent: Fresh-stain rescue: cold milk or potato starch (optional)
homeSolutions.instruction: If the iodine is VERY fresh (under 10 minutes) and you don't have sodium thiosulfate on hand yet: soak the stained area in cold milk for 10 minutes, OR apply a paste of potato starch and cold water and let sit until dry. Both absorb surface iodine. Rinse in cold water. This is a stopgap, not a fix. Still get sodium thiosulfate for the real treatment.

homeSolutions.agent: Sodium thiosulfate solution — the main event
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon of sodium thiosulfate crystals in 1 cup of cool (not warm) water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, press on the stain. Keep the area moist by re-wetting every 2–3 minutes. 8–10 minutes total dwell. Watch the stain fade from orange-brown toward yellow, then colorless as the thiosulfate reduces the iodine to soluble iodide. Blot with a fresh damp cloth between applications.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix sodium thiosulfate with chlorine bleach or strong acid — can generate sulfur dioxide gas. Work in a ventilated area.

homeSolutions.instruction: If a faint brown trace remains after thiosulfate, dab 3% hydrogen peroxide with a clean white cloth. 3–5 minutes dwell, then blot. Peroxide oxidizes any leftover brown compounds. Rinse the thiosulfate out fully before applying peroxide — don't mix them on the fabric. Skip this step on colored or printed cotton — test on the patch first.

Rinse the area with cold water at least 3 times, replacing any under-pad you're using. Residual thiosulfate left on cotton can slowly re-oxidize to cause yellowing over days. Finish with a quick vinegar rinse (1 tbsp white vinegar in 1 cup cold water) for 1 minute to neutralize pH. Air dry — no heat on residual stain.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate if brown persists after full protocol
homeSolutions.instruction: If a brown residue survives the full thiosulfate + peroxide sequence, the iodine has formed deeper complexes with the cellulose that home chemistry won't reach. Tell a pro: "Iodine on cotton, age [x], treated at home with sodium thiosulfate + 3% peroxide. No bleach used. Residual brown remains." They'll run the same chemistry at professional concentration with controlled dwell. Very old brown iodine stains are sometimes permanent even with pro treatment.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Sodium Thiosulfate (Hypo Crystals)
products.note: Professional-grade hypo crystals. Essential for iodine stain removal. Purchase from photography or chemical supply sources. 1 lb lasts for hundreds of treatments.

products.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% USP
products.note: Standard drugstore hydrogen peroxide. Essential backup for residual brown stains. Safe on cotton and most colorfast dyes.

aftercare: Apply dry sodium thiosulfate powder directly to the iodine stain on the dry side. Use a softener brush to work it into the fibers gently. The powder will absorb surface iodine. Do not wet at this stage.
